title: Airtable vs Google Sheets: Which Should You Use?
dek: Choose Google Sheets for free, flexible number-crunching and formulas. Choose Airtable when your spreadsheet has become a database — tasks, projects, or inventory that need linked records and multiple views. Here is the honest breakdown.

seo.metaTitle: Airtable vs Google Sheets: Which to Use?
seo.metaDescription: Airtable vs Google Sheets compared on price, formulas, databases, and views — so you can tell when to use a spreadsheet and when to use Airtable.

richText: Airtable looks like a spreadsheet but works like a database. Google Sheets is a true spreadsheet built for formulas and numbers. They overlap, but they shine at different jobs — and picking the right one saves you a lot of frustration.

items: for calculations, financial models, and free flexible number work.
for organizing structured records — projects, content calendars, inventory, CRMs — with linked tables and views.

body.richText: If your Google Sheet has grown into a tangle of tabs that reference each other — a project tracker, a content pipeline, a product catalog — Airtable replaces that mess with linked tables, and lets the same data appear as a grid, a kanban board, or a calendar. That structure is something a flat spreadsheet cannot match.

richText: For anything driven by formulas — budgets, forecasts, data analysis — Google Sheets is more capable and completely free. It is also simpler to learn. Airtable's free tier has record limits that number-heavy work can hit quickly.

body.richText: A useful rule of thumb: if you mostly calculate, use a spreadsheet; if you mostly organize, use a database like Airtable. Many teams end up using both, for different jobs.

richText: Airtable also appears in our list of

items.question: Is Airtable just a better spreadsheet?
items.answerRichText: Not exactly — it is a database with a spreadsheet-like interface. It is better for organizing linked records and viewing them in different ways, but weaker than Google Sheets for formulas and heavy calculation.

items.question: Is Airtable free?
items.answerRichText: Airtable has a free plan with limits on records per base and features. Larger or more advanced use requires a paid plan, whereas Google Sheets is free for individuals.
